verses.text: David escapes to the cave of Adullam, and to him gather everyone in distress, in debt, or discontented — about four hundred. He becomes captain of the rejected and the desperate. So our greater David still welcomes the troubled and indebted, and makes them His own.

David escapes to the cave of Adullam, and to him gather everyone in distress, in debt, or discontented — about four hundred. He becomes captain of the rejected and the desperate. So our greater David still welcomes the troubled and indebted, and makes them His own.

David sees his aged parents safe in Moab and, when the prophet Gad says return to Judah, he obeys, leaving the safety of the stronghold. Even as a fugitive he honours his parents and heeds God's word. Faith simply does the next right thing, danger or no.

Saul, spear in hand, accuses his servants of conspiring with David, sure that everyone has turned against him. Paranoia is the fruit of a heart that has turned from God; it sees enemies everywhere. The man who fights God's will ends up suspecting all the world.

Doeg the Edomite speaks up, reporting how the priest Ahimelech aided David. The silent witness of the day before now becomes an informer. An evil tongue, biding its time, can turn a small thing into a deadly accusation when it serves its own advancement.

Doeg the Edomite speaks up, reporting how the priest Ahimelech aided David. The silent witness of the day before now becomes an informer. An evil tongue, biding its time, can turn a small thing into a deadly accusation when it serves its own advancement.

Summoned and accused, Ahimelech answers honestly: he had helped David as the king's most faithful servant, knowing nothing of any quarrel. His defense is true and reasonable. But a heart set on blood will not hear reason; innocence is no shield before a tyrant's rage.

Saul orders the priests killed; his guards refuse to lift a hand against the LORD's priests, but Doeg does it — slaying eighty-five priests and putting the whole town of Nob to the sword. Saul who spared the Amalekites now butchers God's priests. Sin, once indulged, knows no limit.

Saul orders the priests killed; his guards refuse to lift a hand against the LORD's priests, but Doeg does it — slaying eighty-five priests and putting the whole town of Nob to the sword. Saul who spared the Amalekites now butchers God's priests. Sin, once indulged, knows no limit.

One priest, Abiathar, escapes to David, who grieves: 'I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house.' He owns his part in their ruin and vows to keep Abiathar safe. True repentance accepts its guilt and turns to protect those it has harmed.
